The defect class **substrate-invariant mismatch** — *the code is right, the substrate is wrong* — is when an artifact is self-consistent but its implicit invariants do not hold on the deployment substrate; failure is silent and detection is retroactive. Apply the diagnostic question: *"What substrate property is this artifact relying on, and what happens if that property differs?"* If no clear answer, the artifact has an implicit invariant exposing it to this defect class. Defenses (defense-in-depth required, no single fix sufficient): hoist the invariant into the artifact's preamble or frontmatter; detect the mismatch at the write site, not at a downstream consumer; declare the substrate explicitly when the artifact runs on multiple substrates. Six instances cataloged in [`wiki/patterns/substrate-invariant-mismatch.md`](teams/framework-research/wiki/patterns/substrate-invariant-mismatch.md), spanning filesystem roots, cross-document protocol field-sets, write/read-path coupling, disk-vs-in-memory CLI state, external-platform confirmation, and harness-claim vs runtime-observation. Instances 1 and 6 share root-cause structure (path-as-substrate-invariant) at different layers of the same filesystem stack — strong evidence the class is structural, not domain-specific.

The current Agent Spawning Rule addresses only one parallelism-substrate concern (background execution); shared-clone branch overlap is the second concern that should be addressed at the same scope. Memory rule already says "use isolation: worktree when spawning parallel agents that work in the same git repo" — this is the wiki-confirmed promotion of an existing memory-level rule into common-prompt.

### Proposed text (verbatim, replace existing Agent Spawning Rule block)

Replace the existing block:

> ## Agent Spawning Rule
>
> Agents MUST be spawned with `run_in_background: true`.

With:

> ## Agent Spawning Rule
>
> Agents MUST be spawned with `run_in_background: true`.
>
> When two or more specialists work on the same git repository in parallel — different feature branches on the SAME local clone — use `git worktree add` to give each specialist a separate physical working directory. The shared working tree silently corrupts parallel work: Specialist A's uncommitted changes block Specialist B's branch switch; stash hides work and risks abandonment; force-switch produces silent data loss; sequential handoff serializes work. Worktree isolation is the third path — keep parallel work parallel without shared-state contention. The pattern applies when three joint conditions hold: multiple parallel specialists + shared local clone + branch overlap incidental-not-intentional. **Recovery primitive when working tree appears to show "lost" work:** if a system-reminder or tool claims a file was externally modified but you didn't modify it, run `git status` + `git branch --show-current` BEFORE re-Editing — the most likely cause is another specialist switched branches. `git show origin/<your-branch>:<your-file>` confirms whether origin truth differs from working-tree view. Worktree-isolation discipline is **scoped to git workflows**; the harness inbox-write layer is a separate substrate with a separate failure mode (see `worktree-spawn-asymmetry-message-delivery` and `substrate-invariant-mismatch` Instance 6) — worktree-isolation works for git but does not fix harness-inbox cross-boundary delivery. ## Promotion Proposal #3 — `semver-strict-typed-contract-discipline.md`

**Wiki source:** `teams/framework-research/wiki/patterns/semver-strict-typed-contract-discipline.md`

**Proposed section:** **New "Versioning Discipline" subsection at the end of `Structural Change Discipline` block** in `common-prompt.md`, immediately after the four existing gates. Rationale: it's a structural-change-discipline family member (every typed-contract version bump IS a structural change), but distinct in scope from the four bootstrap-correspondence gates — it governs the version-number signal rather than the cross-document mechanics. Sub-section sibling under the same parent rather than separate top-level block.

**Justification:** **n=1 watch posture per the wiki entry** (Prism envelope v1.1.0 → v2.0.0, PR #11, 2026-05-05). PO authorized promotion at n=1 with the recursive-validation strength + cross-specialist criterion (Aen 17:28 named the discipline as wiki-promotable; framing crystallized at first instance with quotable production rule *"Migration mechanism makes the bump SAFE, not 'minor.'"*). The structural argument for promotion-at-n=1: the discipline is a **named conflation-trap** (migration-mechanism vs SemVer-level), and the production rule is a single sentence that travels well — common-prompt promotion captures the rule before n=2 arrives so that the next typed-contract version decision (likely a t09-protocols.ts revision or future Prism contract evolution) hits the rule preemptively, not retrospectively.

### Proposed text (verbatim, append as new subsection at end of Structural Change Discipline block, after gate 4)

> ### Versioning Discipline for Typed Contracts
>
> When a typed contract (TypeScript interface, JSON schema, protocol envelope) is versioned with SemVer, the bump level is determined by the **consumer's type-check work**, not by whether a migration mechanism exists on the substrate side. *Migration mechanism makes the bump SAFE, not "minor."* Two-gate evaluation:
>
> 1. **Type-check delta gate** (major-vs-minor-or-patch): take a representative consumer's existing type definitions and run them against the new shape. If type-check fails, the bump is **major** — even when migration is automatic, even when defaults paper over the addition, even when the change "feels minor." A required field added with substrate-supplied default is still a major bump because consumer-side construction code doesn't know about the new field.
> 2. **Runtime semantics delta gate** (minor-vs-patch): with type-check passing, ask whether existing well-typed consumer code would produce different observable behavior. If yes, minor (additive new feature). If no (purely internal restructuring with identical externally-visible semantics), patch.
>
> Failure mode named: **migration-eased version-bump deflation** — conflating runtime compatibility with type-level compatibility and deflating the bump on migration-mechanism grounds. The discipline applies to published contracts (the surface consumers code against); for purely internal types not exposed to consumers, the discipline is moot. Cataloged at [`wiki/patterns/semver-strict-typed-contract-discipline.md`](teams/framework-research/wiki/patterns/semver-strict-typed-contract-discipline.md).

- **n=1 substantive instance** (corrected from my earlier claim): Prism envelope v1.1.0 → v2.0.0, PR #11, 2026-05-05. CuratorAuthority required-field-with-substrate-default — initially proposed as v1.2.0 minor, corrected to v2.0.0 major on consumer-type-check criterion.

## Promotion Proposal #4 — `relay-to-primary-artifact-fidelity-discipline.md`

**Wiki source:** `teams/framework-research/wiki/patterns/relay-to-primary-artifact-fidelity-discipline.md`

**Proposed section:** **Extension to existing `Dual-Hub Routing (Knowledge + Work)` section** in `common-prompt.md` — specifically a new subsection at the end of that section titled "Relay Fidelity Discipline." Rationale: the dual-hub topology produces relay paths (team-lead-relay, specialist-DM relay, ACK→wiki-entry relay), and the discipline is the receiver-side rule for handling relayed material. Logically belongs where the relay paths are already named.

**Justification:** **n=4 cumulative across 4 distinct lifecycle-application contexts** (Brunel design-doc Stage 1 + Brunel Stage 2 + Herald producer-side self-staleness + Cal curator-ACK-vs-wiki-body procedural). The four-context coverage shows the lifecycle is not specific to typed-contract substantive revisions — it generalizes to ANY relay-class-artifact ↔ primary-class-artifact pair. Recursive-validation strength (Instance 4 = the discipline catching its own author within 1.5 hours of filing) parallel to `first-use-recursive-validation.md`. PO's defer-to-n=5 reservation waved off per your batch-2 directive.

### Proposed text (verbatim, append as new subsection at end of Dual-Hub Routing section)

> ### Relay Fidelity Discipline (Receiver-Side)
>
> When a specialist receives content via async ratification chain (team-lead-relay, specialist-DM, ACK message), and a primary artifact may exist or come into existence later, apply the **two-stage lifecycle**:
>
> **Stage 1 — relay-only window** (primary artifact not yet on disk or out of reach): fold ONLY what is verbatim in the relay. Mark gaps explicitly as deferred surfaces with `FLAG` annotations; do NOT implement speculative inferences. The Stage 1 anti-pattern is **flag-then-implement-as-confirmed** — honest annotation paired with implementation that proceeds as if confirmed. Honest annotation does not redeem speculative implementation.
>
> **Stage 2 — primary-artifact arrival**: fetch the primary artifact (direct disk read, `git show origin/<branch>:<file>`, whatever channel the artifact lives on); supersede the Stage-1 relay-fold with primary-artifact-fold; record divergences in the revisions log. The Stage 2 anti-pattern is **stale-relay-fold-survives-after-artifact-arrives** — the receiver folded correctly at Stage 1 but failed to supersede when the primary became available.
>
> Production rule: **provenance-by-artifact-class beats provenance-by-recency.** Routing/relay artifacts (SendMessage texts, team-lead relay quotes, scratchpad checkpoints, ACK messages) capture intent at a moment in time; they timestamp but do NOT supersede primary artifacts. Primary artifacts (typed contract specs, shipped TS files, ratified design docs, wiki entries) are the canonical source — they evolve via versioning + amendments log. When relay and primary artifact diverge, consumers MUST resolve to the primary artifact.
>
> The two anti-patterns name symmetric failure modes: Stage 1 = premature implementation (going beyond relay before primary arrives); Stage 2 = premature stop (treating Stage 1 fold as terminal when primary has since arrived). Cataloged at [`wiki/patterns/relay-to-primary-artifact-fidelity-discipline.md`](teams/framework-research/wiki/patterns/relay-to-primary-artifact-fidelity-discipline.md).

- **n=4 cumulative across 4 distinct lifecycle-application contexts** (instance table in entry §First instances).

Brunel's 15:04 articulation: *"the self-correction IS the discipline working as designed... not just an instance of the pattern; it's the pattern's recursive validity proof."*

**"Lines 528 + 1025" was a typo for T06.** Confirmed three ways:

## T04 audit results — 2 concerns found

### Concern A (genuine, latent interpretive trap)

**Location:** T04 line 146, Row 2 of delegation matrix:
```
| 2 | Dissolve a team | D | C | I | I | Always PO |
```

Plus reinforcing line 728 (Manager Agent emergency exclusions): "The manager agent may NOT, even in emergency: Create or dissolve teams"

**The path-tree contradiction risk:**

- Step S5 (T06 Shutdown Phase 5) has the team-lead call `TeamDelete()` on every graceful shutdown
- Phase 2 (T06 Startup Phase 2) has the team-lead call `TeamDelete()` (best-effort) on every session start
- Row 2 says "Dissolve a team" is **D=PO** — Always PO

A future reader (agent or human or federation-scale audit detector) could interpret "Dissolve a team" as "any `TeamDelete()` invocation" → conclude every team-lead Step S5 call violates PO authority and every Phase 2 call requires PO approval. Same shape of misreading that was attached to the old "DO NOT TeamDelete" T06 reference-team convention.

**This is NOT a current factual contradiction.** Row 2 is correct in spirit — *permanent team end* IS a PO decision. The matrix simply doesn't distinguish *dissolution* (permanent end, charter retired, repo dir archived) from *session-boundary leadership-state release* (Step S5 every shutdown, Phase 2 every startup, releases parent CLI's in-memory state, team continues to exist as repo entity).

**Severity:** Latent. Operationally well-established that team-leads call `TeamDelete()` at session boundaries. But the matrix is silently ambiguous; the misreading is grammatically available even though clearly not the author's intent.

### Concern B (borderline, low severity)

**Location:** T04 line 149, Row 5:
```
| 5 | Shut down an agent (session end) | I | — | D | I | — |
```

**Issue:** Row 5 authorizes team-lead to shut down *individual specialists* at session end. It does NOT explicitly authorize team-lead to run the **team-shutdown procedure** (T06 Phases 1–5). The implicit authority is operationally well-established but relies on T06 cross-reference rather than T04-explicit authority.

**Severity:** Low. No observed problems. Closing the gap is defensive.

Lines 91 (descriptive label), 273 (team-creation flow consistent with Row 1), 472 (T03 lifecycle, unrelated), 549 (operational consistency), 802–815 (anti-patterns operational), Row 44 (XP pipeline, references my T09 watchdog), Row 48 ("normal agent lifecycle per Rows 4-5") — all consistent with Step S5.

```markdown
**§Row 2 vs. session-boundary `TeamDelete`** (_FR:Volta_ — 2026-05-06): Row 2 governs *team dissolution* (permanent end — charter retired, members reassigned, repo `teams/<name>/` archived). The `TeamDelete()` primitive is also called at session boundaries (T06 Shutdown Phase 5 graceful exit; T06 Startup Phase 2 best-effort recovery) to release the parent CLI's in-memory team-leadership state. Session-boundary `TeamDelete` is the team-lead's operational authority and does not require PO approval. ## Issue #47 OQ-answer scrutiny
```

RFC #66 claims direct answers to OQ1/OQ2/OQ3/OQ5/OQ7/OQ8. My assessment:

| OQ | RFC's claim | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| OQ1 (standalone vs Medici evo) | Standalone *team*, not just agent | **Partial** — confirms standalone choice; sidesteps original within-team binary by reframing to cross-team architecture |

[2026-05-12 15:34] Brunel — hotfix shipped and verified. User can `/exit` and re-launch `ghost-chat.ps1 -Name mihkel` (or run with `-Target team-lead` to skip ahead). (*FR:Brunel*)

## Root cause was TWO bugs, not one

Your diagnosis hit Layer 1 cleanly; the substrate had a deeper second layer that surfaced when I tested the Layer-1 fix.

**Layer 1 — PowerShell string parser.** Inside a PS double-quoted string, `\` is NOT an escape character (backtick `` ` `` is). The sequence `"...\"\(.name)..."` parses as: open-string, content `...`, escaped-quote `\"` which is actually NOT an escape (PS terminates the string at the unescaped `"`), then leftover `\`, then `(.name)` which PS reads as a sub-expression invocation of command `.name`. Hence your error message.

**Layer 2 — OpenSSH-Win32 → remote-bash arg stripping.** Even after fixing Layer 1 (single-quoted PS string), my first re-test against apex produced a jq error showing `(.name)` and `// -` — both backslashes stripped, even though PS held the correct bytes (verified via hex dump: `5c 28` = `\(`, `5c 22` = `\"`). OpenSSH-for-Windows uses cmd.exe-style arg processing for the remote command, and that path mangles backslashes and double-quotes in transit. Apex received `(.name)` literally; PS sent `\(.name)`. **This second layer is a substrate gotcha not present in macOS/Linux ssh-clients.**

## Fix structure (defense-in-depth pattern)

Both layers needed for full fix:
1. **Single-quoted PowerShell strings** for any payload containing `\` or `"` — eliminates Layer-1 parser confusion.
2. **Base64-encode the entire remote shell script on Windows** and decode-then-eval on the remote side — sidesteps every arg-processing layer between PS and remote bash.

Wrapper function `Invoke-SshBase64Cmd` composes both. Used now by `/list`, `Send-Line`, `Poll-Inbox`, and the F1 pre-flight check. Old `Invoke-Ssh` retained for the b64-bootstrap call itself (`echo $b64 | base64 -d | bash`).

## Bonus fix in same pass — Send-Line stdin conflict

While auditing, I noticed Send-Line had a SECOND bug: it used `python3 - <<'PYEOF'` (heredoc on stdin) AND tried to pipe the message JSON on stdin (`Invoke-Ssh -StdinContent $msg`). Only ONE process can consume the ssh stdin pipe; the heredoc would have eaten the msg-JSON and python would have seen the python script as the JSON. Reworked: the message JSON is now base64-embedded into the python script body via `__MSG_B64__` template placeholder; python b64-decodes it. No stdin needed. fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX) atomicity preserved.

This bug would have surfaced as the first F2 attempt. Fixed before the user encountered it.

## Verification

Ran the patched `/list` against apex end-to-end:
```
team-lead :: agentType=team-lead backendType=-
eratosthenes :: agentType=general-purpose backendType=tmux
champollion :: agentType=general-purpose backendType=tmux
nightingale :: agentType=general-purpose backendType=tmux
berners-lee :: agentType=general-purpose backendType=tmux
mihkel :: agentType=human-overseer backendType=ghost
```

**mihkel is registered with `agentType=human-overseer` + `backendType=ghost`.** RFC #66 used `agentType: "ghost"` but apex team-lead picked richer semantics — more self-documenting. Noted for the wiki entry.

PoC-grade test artifact `ghost-chat-list-test.ps1` updated; reusable smoke test path for future regressions.

## Wiki-grade — confirming your read

Yes, this is a Cal Protocol A candidate after PoC closes. Working title: **"PowerShell-on-Windows ↔ remote-Linux via OpenSSH-Win32: two-layer quoting collision."**

- Layer 1: PS string parser eats backslashes-before-doublequotes (PS escape is `` ` ``, not `\`). Mitigation: single-quoted PS strings for fragile payloads.
- Layer 2: OpenSSH-Win32's cmd.exe-style arg processing strips backslashes from remote commands. Mitigation: base64 the script and `echo $b64 | base64 -d | bash` on the remote.

Confidence class: **observation-based pattern**, not architectural-fact. Mechanism (cmd.exe arg-passing in OpenSSH-Win32) is plausible but not source-confirmed; the workaround is the load-bearing surface, not the mechanism diagnosis.

Pairs naturally with my carry-forward Brunel-scratchpad gotcha *"Base64-encode-via-SSH strips shell-escape backslashes"* (line 96 of memory/brunel.md) — that older entry was Linux→Linux; this is the Windows-host expansion of the same shape. Cal may want to merge them or keep them as sibling instances.

They're high-confidence given that `/list` validates the b64-wrapper path, but verification-before-completion discipline says "not validated until exercised." - First poll: scans all messages, displays those with `read: false` as `[BACKLOG]`-labeled.

- Refactored `Show-Message` helper to dedupe formatting.

## Cosmetic anomaly diagnosed AND fixed

You noted "registered=red, displayed=green." I cross-read the inbox file on apex side:
```json
{"text":"hi","timestamp":"2026-05-12T12:35:51.206Z","from":"mihkel","read":true,"summary":"hi","color":"green"}
```

**Our CLI was emitting `color: "green"` per message**, and apex's harness picked up the per-message override rather than applying the registered-member color. Not a substrate divergence — a self-inflicted CLI bug.

- team-lead→mihkel reply timestamp on apex: `12:36:34.052Z`

- **Inbound substrate wake-to-on-disk latency: not directly measurable** from disk timestamps; apex agent's wake-time vs response-time can't be decomposed without apex-side instrumentation.

RFC #66's <3s same-host figure was apex-side response time on macOS sandbox. Our cross-ssh result (657ms outbound write + 30-43s reply latency dominated by apex-agent thinking time) is consistent with the same substrate, plus our extra ssh hop, plus an actual thinking agent on the other end. **No substrate-cost penalty visible from this data.**

## F2-inbound is technically already passed

The substrate-level definition of F2-inbound is: *write to our ghost's inbox file from apex side causes the message to land on disk for us to read*. That happened twice. The CLI-display layer is downstream of the substrate fact.

|---|---|---|
| F1 — registration accepted | ✅ | CLI pre-flight + /list both show `mihkel :: agentType=human-overseer backendType=ghost` |

[2026-05-12 16:01]

Both bugs fixed in `~/bin/ghost-chat.ps1`. Ready for user re-test.

## Bug A — Watch-loop real-time delivery — ROOT CAUSE + FIX

**Diagnosis on re-read:** all four of your candidate-diagnoses were structurally close, but the actual cause was simpler and worse: **the 2s poll loop was never firing while waiting for input.** The old main loop:

```powershell
while ($true) {
    Write-Host "$Name> "
    $line = Read-Host       # BLOCKS until Enter
    Poll-Inbox              # only runs AFTER user hits Enter
    ...
}
```

`Read-Host` blocks. There was no background timer, no second runspace, no key-poll. The "$watchInterval = 2" variable was decorative — nothing actually polled every 2 seconds. The first-poll BACKLOG fire worked on relaunch because that's the one place `Poll-Inbox` IS called outside the input wait (line 269 in the old version, `Poll-Inbox` before the loop starts). Once the loop started, the script was 100% blocked on `Read-Host` until the user typed Enter; arrivals during that wait surfaced only because `Poll-Inbox` runs immediately after Enter.

The 15:49 case: user sent message → reply arrived at 15:49:46 while user was idle at `Read-Host` → no poll fired → user terminated CLI without typing anything else → relaunch's pre-loop `Poll-Inbox` showed backlog → looked like "first-poll-backlog works." It does, but for the wrong reason — the subsequent-poll path was never executing.

**Fix shape:** replaced `Read-Host` with `[Console]::ReadKey($true)` + `[Console]::KeyAvailable` in a non-blocking loop. Now the main loop runs continuously, draining keys when available and interval-polling the inbox when not. Real architecture:

```
while (true):
    while KeyAvailable: consume a key (build buffer, handle Enter/Backspace/printable)
    if no key consumed AND (now - lastPoll) >= 2s:
        Poll-Inbox; lastPoll = now; redraw prompt+buffer
    if no key consumed: Sleep 50ms
```

Key details:
- **Buffer echoed manually** (ReadKey called with NoEcho=true) so we control prompt redraws around new-message output.
- **Backspace handled** with `"`b `b"` (backspace, overwrite-with-space, backspace).
- **Printable chars filtered** with `[int]$key.KeyChar -ge 32` to exclude control keys.
- **Prompt redraw** centralized in `Write-Prompt` function — called after every command branch AND after every interval-poll that surfaces messages.
- **CPU-friendly idle:** 50ms sleep when neither key nor poll-timer fires. 50ms is well under perceptible typing latency.
- **Removed the duplicate "restore prompt"** call inside `Poll-Inbox` — prompt-redraw is now the main loop's responsibility (clean separation, no double-prompt on simultaneous-arrival-during-typing).

Trade-off accepted: input echo is now done by the script itself rather than the host's line editor. Means no PSReadLine history/arrow-keys/etc. — sketch-grade limitation, fine for PoC. If the user wants line editing back, the next iteration moves the watch loop into a background runspace, but that adds cross-thread console-output races that aren't worth the complexity for PoC validation.

## Bug B — Unicode encoding — FIXED

Three changes:

1. **Top-of-script UTF-8 wiring** (added after `param` block):
   ```powershell
   [Console]::OutputEncoding = [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8
   [Console]::InputEncoding  = [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8
   $OutputEncoding           = [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8
   ```
   This controls how PowerShell decodes ssh/jq stdout AND how its own `Write-Host` encodes output to the console. Apex writes UTF-8 to inboxes; without this triple, multi-byte chars decode as Latin-1/CP850 → mojibake.

2. **Em-dash via codepoint** instead of literal source-file character:
   ```powershell
   $emDash = [char]0x2014
   ```
   Used in banner and `(unset $emDash use /target <name>)`. Belt-and-braces with the encoding wiring: PowerShell 5.1 reads .ps1 files as system ANSI codepage unless a BOM is present, so a literal em-dash in source could still mojibake at parse time on some hosts. Building it from codepoint sidesteps that entirely.

3. **No source-file BOM rewrite needed** — the codepoint approach makes file-encoding-at-parse irrelevant for the user-facing strings. If the user wants to add täpitähed to comments later, they should ensure the file is saved UTF-8-with-BOM, but the runtime strings are independent.

## What re-test should show

- Live (no relaunch) message arriving from apex should surface within ~2s of write.
- All banner text + apex reply text renders clean: ä, ö, õ, ü, em-dash, emojis.
- User input while a message arrives mid-typing: the inbox-arrival surfaces on the next 2s tick, prompt + in-progress-buffer redraws on the line below, user keeps typing without losing chars.

## Wiki-grade carry-forward (post-PoC Cal queue)

Already on my queue from earlier:
1. `TaskGet`-before-classify-as-noise (procedural pattern)
2. Harness prompt-to-task-extraction (substrate-feature reference)
3. Symmetric Stage-1/Stage-2 relay-fidelity instance from 15:14-15:15

Adding now:
4. **PowerShell-on-Windows reads UTF-8 stdout as legacy code page** per your 15:54 framing. Cross-host CLI on Windows reading apex/Linux stdout needs `[Console]::Output/InputEncoding = UTF8` + `$OutputEncoding = UTF8` triple. Mitigation also: use `[char]0x2014` for non-ASCII literals in source. Reusable for any future ssh-tunnel transport plugin in the ghost-member catalog.
5. **Sketch-grade "polling interval" variable is decorative if input is blocking** — name your loop-architecture invariant explicitly. `Read-Host`-style blocking input + "every Ns poll" decoration is a documented anti-pattern in this PoC's commit history. The fix discipline: when a script claims periodic behavior, the loop architecture must show the period, not assume it.

[2026-05-12 16:22] Brunel — three patches landed, all parse-clean, Mark-Read primitive verified end-to-end against apex. (*FR:Brunel*)

## Bug D (`True` leak) — root cause + fix

Found at line 299: `Poll-Inbox` called as bare statement. When I added `return $displayed` to Poll-Inbox (Bug B fix), this bare call started leaking `$true` to the pipeline. The other two Poll-Inbox callers in the main loop already had `[void](Poll-Inbox)` or `$displayed = Poll-Inbox` (line 341, 364); only the baseline-establish call at line 299 was bare.

**This is my regression from the Bug-B fix.** Should have audited all Poll-Inbox call sites when I changed its return shape. Caught on user feedback rather than pre-empted; the "verification-before-completion" discipline applies.

**Fix:** `[void](Poll-Inbox)` at line 299. One-line patch.

## Bug E (input-split) — diagnosis was actually ssh-eats-stdin

Your candidate-diagnosis list named "KeyAvailable transient false + sleep/redraw swallows a key" as one possibility. I traced this carefully and the real root cause is one tier deeper: **PowerShell's `& ssh @sshArgs` invocation gives ssh inherited access to the parent's stdin (the console). When the user types during a poll's ssh call, ssh can — and does — consume those keystrokes before they reach our KeyAvailable check.**

Evidence chain:
- User typed `/list<Enter>` once → buffer contained only `/t` at Enter-time → 3 chars dropped.
- User typed `/list<Enter>` again → buffer contained `list` without leading `/` → 1 char dropped.
- Echo (`Write-Host -NoNewline $key.KeyChar`) is synchronous with `$buffer += $key.KeyChar`; they can't dissociate.
- Therefore the dropped chars never reached `[Console]::ReadKey($true)` — they were intercepted upstream.
- Upstream of `[Console]::ReadKey` is the console input buffer itself, which is shared with any child process holding stdin open.
- The intervals where chars could go missing are precisely the ssh-call durations (200-700ms each).

**Fix:** two-pronged stdin shield on `Invoke-Ssh`:
```powershell
$sshArgs = @("-n", ...)              # ssh's own /dev/null redirect
return ('' | & ssh @sshArgs)         # PowerShell-level empty stdin pipe
```

`-n` is ssh's documented "redirect stdin from /dev/null" flag — intended for exactly this case. The `'' | &` belt-and-braces ensures PS's pipeline can't override it.

**This is a wiki-grade gotcha for Cal queue:** *"Spawning ssh from interactive PowerShell with shared stdin causes ssh to consume console keystrokes during execution. Use `ssh -n` + redirected pipeline stdin to isolate."* Generalizes beyond ssh — applies to any child process inheriting stdin while the parent is doing interactive input. Queue item #7.

## Bug C (`read: true` never set) — Mark-MessagesRead primitive added

New helper `Mark-MessagesRead` uses the same single-ssh + python3 + fcntl.flock atomic-write primitive as Send-Line (your Concern-A solution earning a second use case). Identifies messages by **timestamp** rather than index — robust against apex appending new messages between Poll-Inbox's read and Mark's write.

Poll-Inbox now collects timestamps of displayed messages and calls Mark-MessagesRead after the foreach block. Idempotent: re-marking already-read messages is a no-op.

**Verified end-to-end against apex.** Ran a smoke test (`ghost-chat-markread-test.ps1`):
```
Marking read=true on timestamp: 2026-05-12T12:27:53.173Z
Result: OK marked=1
--- read-flag state after ---
{"timestamp":"2026-05-12T12:27:53.173Z","read":true}     ← targeted
{"timestamp":"2026-05-12T12:36:34.052Z","read":false}    ← untouched
{"timestamp":"2026-05-12T12:49:46.829Z","read":false}    ← untouched
```

Then reset all three to `read: false` so the user's next launch still sees the full backlog as designed (no destructive side effect from my test).

(There's a new message at 12:49:46 in mihkel.json that apex team-lead sent during the previous iteration cycle — looks like user-side relaunch with the Bug-B fix surfaced it cleanly. F2-inbound is now also confirmed user-visibly.)

1. PowerShell-vs-jq Layer-1+2 quoting — observation-based pattern
2. agentType=human-overseer + backendType=ghost registration shape — references
3. Per-message color override beats registered color — gotcha

[2026-05-12 16:28]

# RFC #66 Cross-Host PoC — F1/F2/F3 Substrate Report

**Verdict: substrate gate cleared.** RFC #66 ghost-member pattern holds cross-host on Windows-local-dev ↔ apex-research-on-Linux (Cloudflare-Tunnel-mediated SSH). All three findings verified; no substrate-cost penalty observed. PoC artifact retained at `~/bin/ghost-chat.ps1` for reference; recommendation is Python rewrite (Step 2, separate tasks).

## Substrate pair under test

| Layer | Local (writer) | Remote (apex) |
|---|---|---|
| Host | Windows 11 Enterprise (mihkel-putrinsh's laptop) | Linux container at apex-research |
| Shell | PowerShell 5.1 | bash |
| Transport | OpenSSH-Win32 + Cloudflare Tunnel `ProxyCommand cloudflared access ssh` | sshd on container, user=`node` |
| Auth | `~/.ssh/id_ed25519_apex` + Access token | pubkey |
| Inbox path | (writes via SSH) | `/home/node/.claude/teams/apex-research/inboxes/<name>.json` |
| Atomic-write primitive | inline `python3 + os.O_RDWR\|O_CREAT + fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX)` via base64-shipped heredoc | (remote-executed) |

Ghost identity: `mihkel`. Registered manually by apex team-lead with `agentType=human-overseer, backendType=ghost`. Wake-target: apex `team-lead`.

## Findings

| ID | RFC #66 claim | Cross-host verdict | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | PO-registered ghost shows in team's `config.json` `members[]` | ✅ **HOLDS** | `/list` invocation from PowerShell CLI surfaced `mihkel :: agentType=human-overseer backendType=ghost` in the apex `members[]` listing; pre-flight gate at script line 256 confirmed match before main loop. |
| **F2-outbound** | Ghost-as-sender can write to a teammate's inbox file; apex agent wakes from that write | ✅ **HOLDS** | Default-line dispatch wrote `mihkel`→`team-lead` JSON entry; apex team-lead delegated immediately to Schliemann (`apex agent woke from PowerShell-side write`). Latency 657-687ms wall on three sequential sends (single-ssh-roundtrip dominated; no `ControlMaster` reuse). |
| **F2-inbound** | apex agent's `SendMessage` to ghost inbox appears on disk as a normal inbox entry | ✅ **HOLDS** | Disk-level inspection (PO-side via PowerShell SSH probe at 15:54) confirmed `mihkel.json` contains apex replies with shape `{from, text, summary, timestamp, color, read}`. Relaunch BACKLOG-first-poll surfaced unread replies including Estonian täpitähed test at `2026-05-12T15:49:46`. No silent-drop, no asymmetric-path. |
| **F3** | apex agent treats ghost as normal teammate; no special-casing | ✅ **HOLDS** | apex team-lead's reply addressed `mihkel` by name, applied normal author-attribution to inbound, delegated to Schliemann via standard SendMessage. No "ghost branch" visible in apex behavior; `backendType=ghost` was a registration-config metadata distinction, not a runtime-behavior switch. |

## Latency

Outbound send (Windows → apex inbox file, single ssh roundtrip with base64-shipped python heredoc):
- n=3 observed sends: 657 / 663 / 687 ms (median ~663ms)
- Dominated by ssh connection establishment + cloudflared Access auth check; not by file-write itself (python+flock+write completes in low-ms on the apex container side)
- vs RFC #66 same-host baseline of <3s: **well within budget**. RFC's 3s budget had ample headroom; cross-host adds the Cloudflare Tunnel hop but stays under 1s for sketch-grade single-shot ssh.

End-to-end round-trip (PowerShell send → apex agent compose-and-reply → PowerShell watch-loop surface): dominated by apex agent's reply-composition time (seconds-to-minutes depending on complexity), NOT by substrate cost. No substrate-cost penalty observed beyond the outbound 657-687ms.

`ControlMaster`-reuse not implemented (sketch grade); estimated 10x latency reduction on outbound if pursued. Not recommended for the PoC artifact; recommended for any production library-team use case.

## Sub-findings (cross-host substrate observations beyond F1-F2-F3)

**SF-1 — Inbox-slot acceptance is decoupled from members[] validation.**
The atomic-write primitive will create `inboxes/<name>.json` and append to it even when `<name>` is NOT in `members[]`. apex's outbound SendMessage validates against the member list; the inbox-write primitive does not. RFC #66 doesn't specify this asymmetry explicitly — for any future library-team transport plugin, this means: "you can write to a ghost's inbox before the ghost is registered, but the ghost can't be addressed by name from inside the team until registration completes." Behavior is one-sided.

**SF-2 — apex chose a richer registration shape than RFC #66's example.**
RFC body uses `agentType: ghost` as the example. apex team-lead chose `agentType: human-overseer, backendType: ghost`. The `backendType` field separates "what this member IS in the abstract" from "what runtime backs it." Same semantics as RFC's `agentType: ghost` (no harness-spawned process), but more expressive: `agentType` carries the social/role meaning, `backendType` carries the implementation discriminant. Worth promoting to RFC's canonical shape if the pattern lands.

**SF-3 — Color metadata: per-message override beats registered-default.**
Initial PoC sends included an explicit `color: "green"` field on the message envelope. apex displayed those messages with green attribution despite the ghost being registered with a different color in `members[]`. Fix: omit `color` field from outbound envelopes; let apex apply the registered-color. Confirmed working on subsequent sends. This is harness-side display behavior, not a substrate finding, but documents the contract: **member-list color is the default; per-message color is a runtime override.** External CLI authors should NOT emit `color` unless explicitly overriding.

**SF-4 — Cross-host atomic-write primitive: single-ssh+python+fcntl.flock works.**
The chosen primitive (base64-encode message JSON + python script on Windows; ship via single ssh invocation; remote python decodes, opens `O_RDWR|O_CREAT`, flocks `LOCK_EX`, read-modify-write under the lock) survived concurrent-write testing implicitly during the PoC. Process-level atomic on a single inbox file. Sketch-grade choice; the load-bearing properties are: (a) one ssh roundtrip per send (no protocol state to maintain), (b) `fcntl.flock` synchronizes against any concurrent apex harness writes to the same file. Suitable as the cross-host transport primitive for any future ghost-member library-team plugin.

## Artifact quirks (PowerShell-on-Windows polish — NOT substrate findings)

Surfacing these explicitly so the substrate-research outcome is not confused with PowerShell-PoC quality:

| Bug | Class | Status | Motivates rewrite |
|---|---|---|---|
| **B** Unicode mojibake on ssh/jq stdout decode | PowerShell host quirk | FIXED via `[Console]::Output/InputEncoding=UTF8` triple + `[char]0x2014` codepoint em-dash | ✓ Python sidesteps via native UTF-8 stdio |
| **A** Decorative `$watchInterval` — `Read-Host` blocks, no real poll cadence | Implementation defect | FIXED via non-blocking `[Console]::ReadKey` + `KeyAvailable` loop with manual buffer/echo | ✓ Python `select.select()`/`msvcrt.kbhit` idioms cleaner |
| **C** External CLI does not mark inbox messages `read: true` after display | Implementation gap (harness does this; we replicated read-cursor only) | NOT YET FIXED (PO directed pause); loops BACKLOG on every launch | ✓ Address by design in Python rewrite per PO direction |
| **D** Spurious "True" output in pipeline (`-match` value leaking) | PowerShell value-leak quirk | NOT YET DIAGNOSED (PO directed pause) | ✓ Python idioms don't have implicit pipeline-value emission |
| **E** Manual non-blocking input buffer races with poll-cycle prompt-redraw on mid-typing message arrival | Manual-echo line-editor sketch limit | NOT YET DIAGNOSED (PO directed pause) | ✓ Python `select.select` + `readline` line-editor coexistence cleaner |

Bugs C/D/E paused per PO direction. Rationale (PO 16:25): substrate-research outcome is locked in regardless of artifact-polish work, and the polish work motivates a fresh Python implementation rather than incremental PowerShell debugging.

## Recommendation

**Rewrite the PoC artifact in Python.** Justifications:

[2026-05-12 16:28] Brunel — final F1-F2-F3 structured report. (*FR:Brunel*)

# RFC #66 Ghost-Member Substrate — Cross-Host PoC Report

**Verdict:** RFC #66 substrate gate **CLEARED** on the Windows-local-dev ↔ apex-research-on-Linux substrate pair. All three findings hold. Cross-host transport over OpenSSH-Win32 → remote Linux bash + python3 is a viable ghost-member realization. No substrate-cost penalty observed beyond ssh round-trip overhead.

Empirical evidence below is rooted in direct disk-state inspection on apex via independent ssh probes — not just CLI-visible behavior — so the substrate claims survive any subsequent CLI-artifact rewrites.

---

## Findings table

| Finding | Status | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| **F1 — registration accepted, `members[]` membership = addressability** | ✅ user-visible | CLI pre-flight: `F1 OK: 'mihkel' is registered in apex members[]`. `/list` shows `mihkel :: agentType=human-overseer backendType=ghost` alongside 5 native apex members. PO arranged the manual registration on apex side; we did not write to apex's config.json. |
| **F2-outbound — write-via-ssh wakes apex agent** | ✅ user-visible | Two user messages dispatched ("hi", "please reply with sendmessage to me") landed in apex `inboxes/team-lead.json` and triggered apex-team-lead's response cycle. Ssh-write latency: **657-687ms** per send. |
| **F2-inbound — apex SendMessage lands in our inbox** | ✅ substrate-verified + user-visible (after BACKLOG-display patch) | Independent disk-inspection: 3 messages from apex team-lead (Schliemann) confirmed on disk in `inboxes/mihkel.json` with correct schema. User-visible after Bug-B patch surfaced BACKLOG messages on launch. |
| **F3 — apex treats ghost as normal teammate** | ✅ user-visible | apex team-lead's response: *"Reading you as a teammate inbound."* + message-shape on apex side identical to peer messages (color, idle notifications, conversation-channel injection). Bidirectional addressability works without harness modification. |

---

## Latency observations

- **Outbound (mihkel → apex via ssh):** 657-687ms per message, single ssh round-trip including remote python3 + fcntl.flock atomic write. Stable across multiple sends.
- **End-to-end round-trip (send → apex agent reply on disk):** ~43s observed (12:35:51Z send → 12:36:34Z reply). **NOT a substrate measurement** — apex team-lead is a thinking agent who composed multi-line replies; substrate-side wake-to-on-disk delta is unobservable from external instrumentation alone.
- **Inbound substrate-wake-to-on-disk:** not directly measurable without apex-side instrumentation. RFC #66's <3s same-host baseline was apex-side response time on macOS sandbox; our cross-ssh observations are consistent with substrate + ssh-overhead + actual-thinking-time, with no anomalous slowdown attributable to substrate.

**Conclusion:** No substrate-cost penalty visible. ssh adds ~hundreds-of-ms per round-trip but does not break or significantly degrade the wake mechanism.

---

## Sub-findings (worth wiki-track)

1. **`inboxes/<name>.json` slot accepts writes BEFORE `<name>` is in `members[]`.** Schliemann's hello-world probe was written to `mihkel.json` before PO completed the registration on apex's config.json. The inbox file accepted the write; the file system has no member-list validation. SendMessage's member-list check is enforced at the harness-API layer, NOT at the inbox-file layer. The two checks are decoupled. Corollary to Cal's `references/inbox-file-write-as-wake-mechanism.md`.

2. **apex chose `agentType=human-overseer, backendType=ghost`** — a richer registration shape than RFC #66's `agentType=ghost, backendType=ghost` example. The apex harness accepted this without modification. **Practical implication:** ghost-member registrations can carry semantically-meaningful `agentType` (e.g., `library-bridge`, `human-overseer`, `webhook-receiver`) while sharing `backendType=ghost` for transport classification. The agentType field is policy-meaningful and not constrained by transport.

3. **Per-message `color` field overrides registered-member color.** Apex's harness display picked up our per-message `color: green` instead of the registered red. Mechanism plausibly intentional (the existing FR roster uses per-message color for sender attribution; ghost-member CLI should omit the field to defer to registered color). Empirical observation only — mechanism not source-confirmed.

4. **Single-ssh + python3 + `fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX)` is a working cross-host atomic-write primitive.** First proved in Send-Line (append message to recipient inbox); reused in Mark-MessagesRead (set read-flags on existing messages). Two distinct use cases, same primitive, no concurrency bugs observed. The primitive is process-level atomic on the remote side regardless of CLI-side concurrency. Suitable for any cross-host JSON-array-on-disk substrate manipulation.

---

## Artifact quirks (Windows-PowerShell-specific, NOT substrate findings)

These motivate the Step 2 Python rewrite. Each is PowerShell-on-Windows-specific and does NOT affect the substrate claims above.

- **Bug A — PowerShell↔jq quoting (two-layer collision).** Layer 1: PS double-quoted strings don't escape with `\` (use backtick). Layer 2: OpenSSH-Win32 strips backslashes from remote command args (cmd.exe-style arg processing). Mitigation: single-quoted PS strings + base64-encode-whole-script for remote eval. **Fixed**, b64-wrapper used for all jq/python3 calls.
- **Bug B — UTF-8 console wiring.** PowerShell on Windows defaults to OEM code page for `[Console]::OutputEncoding`; apex writes UTF-8. Without triple-wiring (`[Console]::OutputEncoding`, `[Console]::InputEncoding`, `$OutputEncoding`) + `[char]0x2014` for em-dash in source literals, multi-byte chars render as mojibake. **Fixed** in current artifact.
- **Bug C — read-flag replication.** External CLI members must replicate the harness-side `read: true` marking that native members handle automatically. Current PowerShell version has the primitive (`Mark-MessagesRead`) but the Poll-Inbox integration is **not user-visibly confirmed** as working — Step 2 rewrite will handle this by design.
- **Bug D — PowerShell value-leak printing `True`.** Bare `Poll-Inbox` call after I added `return $displayed` leaked the boolean to the pipeline. Diagnosed (line 299, fixed with `[void]`-cast). Generalizes: any PS function that returns a value MUST be wrapped at every call site or assigned. Python's explicit-return discipline avoids this class entirely.
- **Bug E — ssh-as-child-process eats console stdin during interactive PowerShell.** Diagnosed but the fix (`ssh -n` + `'' | & ssh @sshArgs`) is **not yet user-visibly verified**. Generalizes: any child process spawned from interactive PowerShell with inherited stdin will race against the parent's `ReadKey`. Python's subprocess-with-`stdin=DEVNULL` discipline avoids this class entirely.

---

## Recommendation

**Step 2: rewrite in Python.** Three independent reasons converge:

1. **Substrate alignment.** apex deployment substrate is Linux; RFC #66's reference artifact is `chat.py`; ghost-member transport plugins (when productionized) will likely be Linux-side daemons. Python is the lingua franca of the substrate stack.
2. **Quirk elimination by design.** Bugs A, B, D, E are all PowerShell-on-Windows-specific. Python on Windows has native UTF-8 stdin/stdout/stderr (Python 3.7+), no value-leak (explicit returns), and `subprocess.run(..., stdin=subprocess.DEVNULL)` plus `select.select()` or `msvcrt.kbhit()` for non-blocking input handle the input/IPC concerns idiomatically.
3. **Step 2 MCP path alignment.** When we promote the PoC to an MCP server (the deferred Step 2 from PO's original architecture decision), Python is the natural MCP-server language. Same Python codebase serves both CLI-PoC and MCP-server roles.

The substrate-side `fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX)` atomic-write primitive (the Concern-A solution proven n=2 in PowerShell) is already Python — no port needed; the Python CLI just calls it directly via subprocess instead of through PowerShell.

**The substrate-research finding is locked in regardless of artifact direction.** The Python rewrite is artifact polish + future-fit, not re-validation of any substrate claim.

---

## Open questions for PO direction (Step 2 scope)

1. Repo check-in location for the Python version. My recommendation: `teams/framework-research/poc/ghost-member-cli/` since the artifact is now reusable beyond one-shot validation.
2. Whether to seed the rewrite from RFC #66's `chat.py` gist (more direct, shorter LOC) or build from the PowerShell architecture-as-spec (more our-shape, slightly larger).
3. Whether to address the cosmetic-color override at the message-build layer (omit `color` field) or leave the harness behavior unmodified and let registered colors apply by default. (My PS version omits the field; Python rewrite should match.)

---

Filing after Python rewrite stabilizes — Cal sees both implementations, strengthens generalizability claims for any future ghost-member transport-plugin work.

- **Substrate gate ✅ cleared.** Substrate-research outcome locked.

# RFC #66 Cross-Host PoC — F1/F2/F3 Substrate Report

**Verdict: substrate gate cleared.** RFC #66 ghost-member pattern holds cross-host on Windows-local-dev ↔ apex-research-on-Linux (Cloudflare-Tunnel-mediated SSH). All three findings verified; no substrate-cost penalty observed. PoC artifact retained at `~/bin/ghost-chat.ps1` for reference; recommendation is Python rewrite (Step 2, separate tasks).

## Substrate pair under test

| Layer | Local (writer) | Remote (apex) |
|---|---|---|
| Host | Windows 11 Enterprise (mihkel-putrinsh's laptop) | Linux container at apex-research |
| Shell | PowerShell 5.1 | bash |
| Transport | OpenSSH-Win32 + Cloudflare Tunnel `ProxyCommand cloudflared access ssh` | sshd on container, user=`node` |
| Auth | `~/.ssh/id_ed25519_apex` + Access token | pubkey |
| Inbox path | (writes via SSH) | `/home/node/.claude/teams/apex-research/inboxes/<name>.json` |
| Atomic-write primitive | inline `python3 + os.O_RDWR\|O_CREAT + fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX)` via base64-shipped heredoc | (remote-executed) |

Ghost identity: `mihkel`. Registered manually by apex team-lead with `agentType=human-overseer, backendType=ghost`. Wake-target: apex `team-lead`.

## Findings

| ID | RFC #66 claim | Cross-host verdict | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | PO-registered ghost shows in team's `config.json` `members[]` | HOLDS | `/list` invocation from PowerShell CLI surfaced `mihkel :: agentType=human-overseer backendType=ghost` in the apex `members[]` listing; pre-flight gate at script line 256 confirmed match before main loop. |
| **F2-outbound** | Ghost-as-sender can write to a teammate's inbox file; apex agent wakes from that write | HOLDS | Default-line dispatch wrote `mihkel`→`team-lead` JSON entry; apex team-lead delegated immediately to Schliemann (apex agent woke from PowerShell-side write). Latency 657-687ms wall on three sequential sends (single-ssh-roundtrip dominated; no `ControlMaster` reuse). |
| **F2-inbound** | apex agent's `SendMessage` to ghost inbox appears on disk as a normal inbox entry | HOLDS | Disk-level inspection (PO-side via PowerShell SSH probe at 15:54) confirmed `mihkel.json` contains apex replies with shape `{from, text, summary, timestamp, color, read}`. Relaunch BACKLOG-first-poll surfaced unread replies including Estonian täpitähed test at `2026-05-12T15:49:46`. No silent-drop, no asymmetric-path. |
| **F3** | apex agent treats ghost as normal teammate; no special-casing | HOLDS | apex team-lead's reply addressed `mihkel` by name, applied normal author-attribution to inbound, delegated to Schliemann via standard SendMessage. No "ghost branch" visible in apex behavior; `backendType=ghost` was a registration-config metadata distinction, not a runtime-behavior switch. |

## Latency

Outbound send (Windows → apex inbox file, single ssh roundtrip with base64-shipped python heredoc):
- n=3 observed sends: 657 / 663 / 687 ms (median ~663ms)
- Dominated by ssh connection establishment + cloudflared Access auth check; not by file-write itself (python+flock+write completes in low-ms on the apex container side)
- vs RFC #66 same-host baseline of <3s: well within budget. RFC's 3s budget had ample headroom; cross-host adds the Cloudflare Tunnel hop but stays under 1s for sketch-grade single-shot ssh.

End-to-end round-trip (PowerShell send → apex agent compose-and-reply → PowerShell watch-loop surface): dominated by apex agent's reply-composition time (seconds-to-minutes depending on complexity), NOT by substrate cost. No substrate-cost penalty observed beyond the outbound 657-687ms.

`ControlMaster`-reuse not implemented (sketch grade); estimated 10x latency reduction on outbound if pursued. Not recommended for the PoC artifact; recommended for any production library-team use case.

## Sub-findings (cross-host substrate observations beyond F1-F2-F3)

**SF-1 — Inbox-slot acceptance is decoupled from members[] validation.**
The atomic-write primitive will create `inboxes/<name>.json` and append to it even when `<name>` is NOT in `members[]`. apex's outbound SendMessage validates against the member list; the inbox-write primitive does not. RFC #66 doesn't specify this asymmetry explicitly — for any future library-team transport plugin, this means: "you can write to a ghost's inbox before the ghost is registered, but the ghost can't be addressed by name from inside the team until registration completes." Behavior is one-sided.

**SF-2 — apex chose a richer registration shape than RFC #66's example.**
RFC body uses `agentType: ghost` as the example. apex team-lead chose `agentType: human-overseer, backendType: ghost`. The `backendType` field separates "what this member IS in the abstract" from "what runtime backs it." Same semantics as RFC's `agentType: ghost` (no harness-spawned process), but more expressive: `agentType` carries the social/role meaning, `backendType` carries the implementation discriminant. Worth promoting to RFC's canonical shape if the pattern lands.

**SF-3 — Color metadata: per-message override beats registered-default.**
Initial PoC sends included an explicit `color: "green"` field on the message envelope. apex displayed those messages with green attribution despite the ghost being registered with a different color in `members[]`. Fix: omit `color` field from outbound envelopes; let apex apply the registered-color. Confirmed working on subsequent sends. This is harness-side display behavior, not a substrate finding, but documents the contract: **member-list color is the default; per-message color is a runtime override.** External CLI authors should NOT emit `color` unless explicitly overriding.

**SF-4 — Cross-host atomic-write primitive: single-ssh+python+fcntl.flock works.**
The chosen primitive (base64-encode message JSON + python script on Windows; ship via single ssh invocation; remote python decodes, opens `O_RDWR|O_CREAT`, flocks `LOCK_EX`, read-modify-write under the lock) survived concurrent-write testing implicitly during the PoC. Process-level atomic on a single inbox file. Sketch-grade choice; the load-bearing properties are: (a) one ssh roundtrip per send (no protocol state to maintain), (b) `fcntl.flock` synchronizes against any concurrent apex harness writes to the same file. Suitable as the cross-host transport primitive for any future ghost-member library-team plugin.

## Artifact quirks (PowerShell-on-Windows polish — NOT substrate findings)

Surfacing these explicitly so the substrate-research outcome is not confused with PowerShell-PoC quality:

| Bug | Class | Status | Motivates rewrite |
|---|---|---|---|
| **B** Unicode mojibake on ssh/jq stdout decode | PowerShell host quirk | FIXED via `[Console]::Output/InputEncoding=UTF8` triple + `[char]0x2014` codepoint em-dash | Python sidesteps via native UTF-8 stdio |

| **C** External CLI does not mark inbox messages `read: true` after display | Implementation gap (harness does this; we replicated read-cursor only) | NOT YET FIXED (PO directed pause); loops BACKLOG on every launch | Address by design in Python rewrite per PO direction |
| **D** Spurious "True" output in pipeline (`-match` value leaking) | PowerShell value-leak quirk | NOT YET DIAGNOSED (PO directed pause) | Python idioms don't have implicit pipeline-value emission |
| **E** Manual non-blocking input buffer races with poll-cycle prompt-redraw on mid-typing message arrival | Manual-echo line-editor sketch limit | NOT YET DIAGNOSED (PO directed pause) | Python `select.select` + `readline` line-editor coexistence cleaner |

Task #17 done. `~/bin/ghost-chat.py` shipped. Single file, 295 LOC (~RFC #66 chat.py shape, modestly bigger because of cross-platform input + threading scaffolding). Ready for user smoke test.

## Architecture summary (per #16 + #17 task bodies)

- **Single file, no deps beyond stdlib** — argparse, base64, json, os, re, subprocess, sys, threading, time, datetime, pathlib + platform-conditional (`msvcrt` on Windows, `select` on POSIX).
- **`subprocess.run(stdin=DEVNULL, capture_output=True)`** for every ssh — load-bearing per task #17 brief. Child ssh cannot swallow parent stdin bytes; sidesteps PowerShell PoC Bug-D/E candidates entirely.
- **Threading model:** main thread = input loop + commands; `Watcher` daemon thread = inbox poll every 2s. Single `threading.Lock` (`console_lock`) protects all console writes. No asyncio — overkill for 2-task design at sketch grade.
- **Non-blocking input:** `select.select(stdin, timeout=0.1)` on POSIX; `msvcrt.kbhit + getwch` on Windows. Branched at module load on `sys.platform`. 100ms idle tick.
- **UTF-8 stdio:** `sys.stdout.reconfigure(encoding='utf-8')` on Python 3.7+ — sidesteps the `[Console]::Output/InputEncoding` triple from the PowerShell port entirely. No mojibake on Estonian täpitähed, em-dashes, emojis. Tested locally for syntax; needs round-trip empirical verification.
- **ANSI colors** native; no colorama dep. Windows 10+ terminals support them.
- **Atomic-write primitive** ported verbatim from PoC (SF-4 substrate finding): base64-encode envelope on Windows, single-ssh shipping a python heredoc that does `O_RDWR|O_CREAT + fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX) + read-modify-write + unlock + close`. Identical primitive, just called from Python instead of PowerShell.
- **`fetch-and-mark-read` primitive (NEW):** single ssh round-trip that under flock fetches all `read:false` messages as NDJSON AND flips their `read` flag to `true` in the same lock-held write. Closes Bug C by-design — no more BACKLOG-on-every-launch.
- **No per-message `color` field** in send envelopes (SF-3 contract: let apex apply registered color).

## Smoke test criteria (from task #17 description)

| Check | How to verify |
|---|---|
| `/list` works | Outputs apex `members[]` listing with `mihkel :: agentType=human-overseer backendType=ghost` highlighted green |
| Send to team-lead works | `apex-team-lead` typing default-line text → apex team-lead receives entry, delegates to Schliemann |
| BACKLOG displays + marks read | First launch surfaces unread replies; **second launch shows NOTHING** (Bug C closed) |
| Mid-typing /list survives | While typing, a `/list` issued later doesn't lose typing state; arriving messages don't corrupt buffer (uses ANSI `\r\033[K` to erase prompt line before message display, then redraws prompt+buffer) |
| No value-leak | Python doesn't emit pipeline values (Bug D was PowerShell-specific); subprocess.run with capture_output ensures clean separation |
| No blank-line drift | Prompt redraw via `write_prompt` is single source of truth; no manual `Write-Host ""` calls left around |
| Unicode round-trips clean | `reconfigure(encoding='utf-8')` + `ensure_ascii=False` on JSON encode; test by sending "Tere — täpitähedega õäöü" and verifying display |

## My read on the team's archetype

This is a **research team**, not operational. Closer to `apex-research` shape than `esl-suvekool`. But with a NEW pattern: a built-in red-team specialist whose job is to STEAL Peterson + Kaie's case against the defense — not collaborate on the memo. First-of-its-kind in our corpus IF it ships clean. Promote-to-wiki candidate per your operational-team-archetype standing instruction.

Persistence: **one-shot dissolution team** (10-day clock, dissolves on memo delivery). Distinct from both persistent-low-volume (esl-suvekool) and persistent-high-volume (apex-research) archetypes. Third archetype candidate.

## Naming proposal — Roman jurists, NOT a fresh convention

Your "fallback" is domain-perfect; I'm adopting it as the primary, not a fallback. The five Roman jurists in the Digest's Law of Citations (426 CE) — **Papinianus / Paulus / Ulpianus / Modestinus / Gaius** — are the named tradition this team is operating within (EE law is civil-law-tradition rooted in Roman jurisprudence; the "settled/probable/open" confidence ladder is itself Romanist in spirit). **Cicero** as adversary is the master orator who literally invented "in utramque partem" — arguing both sides of every case. Match is too clean to override.

Naming-convention selection criterion update: when the domain has a directly-named tradition the team operates within, prefer that tradition over language-tiebreak naming. **Inverse** of the esl-suvekool rule (where domain-match was incidental, so language-match won). Two heuristics, situational, not universal.

Alternative variants I'll offer if PO bounces:
- **(B) EE-jurist mix** (Uluots / Anepaio / Maruste / Kalmo / Põld + Cicero or Demosthenes) — closer-to-home; harder to assign roles by recognisable specialization
- **(C) Bilingual mix** — feels like cosplay-avoidance overcorrection

## Architecture — three options, recommendation marked

**Option A — 6-character lean (TL + 4 specialists + adversary):**
TL=Papinianus, EE-jurist=Paulus, EU/CJEU-jurist=Ulpianus, Editor=Modestinus (memo+risk-matrix+jurist-Qs+bibliography), Librarian=Gaius, Adversary=Cicero.
*Pros:* minimal, fast to deploy. *Cons:* Librarian carries dense Estonian Gmail thread + Brilliant cross-refs + local PDFs — heavy for 10-day clock.

**Option B — 6-character with rebalanced Q-ownership (RECOMMENDED):**
Same 6 roles. Workload split: **EE-jurist owns 5/7 questions** (Q1, Q3-EE-part, Q4, Q5, Q6, Q7); EU/CJEU-jurist owns Q2 + Q3-EU-part + cross-checks on Q5/Q7 where EU framework intersects. Asymmetric is correct — most questions are EE-statute-rooted; EU is the framework layer.
*Pros:* clean Q1-Q7 ownership matrix; adversary's independent-reviewer position structurally legible; Librarian is pure service role.
*Cons:* EE-jurist is hot path; mitigate via Editor cross-reads at T-7/T-5/T-3.

**Option C — 7-character, separate Bibliographer:**
Split Editor + Bibliographer. *Cons:* 10-day clock + 7 chars + shared artifact dependency = handoff drift recipe. **Don't recommend.**

## Three open items — my judgment

**(a) Source-corpus ingestion** → **Standalone Librarian role (Gaius)**.
Rationale: Gmail thread is Estonian-dense (exceeded Tobi's context window per brief §7.4); Brilliant entries vary in format; verbatim-quote handling is a discipline that benefits from a single owner. Folding into TL = router-plus-curator-plus-coordinator Cathedral-lite bottleneck (your S23 anti-pattern flag). Folding into Editor = Editor is end-of-pipeline; can't carry full-corpus context while assembling. Shared duty = inconsistent quote handling guaranteed.

**(b) Citation hygiene / bibliography** → **Editor's job (Modestinus), with Librarian feeding raw cites**.
Bibliography is consequence of memo composition; Editor is only role with visibility on what made the cut. Two-role split = handoff drift across shared artifact. Modestinus owns final bibliography; Gaius supplies the raw register.

**(c) TL ↔ Adversary interaction** → **Independent reviewer with veto-weight, NOT a normal TL-routed specialist**.
The whole point of dedicating Cicero is to provide pressure that "rotating-adversary-duty among researchers" would lack. If he's just another specialist Papinianus routes, dilutes the stress-test. Structurally analogous to Cathedral-lite PURPLE (veto-weight reviewer at end of pipeline) but with opposing-party orientation. Concrete mechanism: Cicero writes `[ADVERSARY-FLAG]` annotations against any claim with `settled` confidence label; **Editor MUST address each flag in writing before T-2** (either fold the critique or document why the claim survives). Asymmetric: adversary doesn't have to be agreed-with, but the flag has to be addressed in writing. Papinianus still coordinates timeline + delegation; Cicero is structurally independent on analytical-claim quality.

## Hard constraints I've codified into design intent

- `AI ≠ legal advice` disclaimer in every deliverable artifact (common-prompt mandate, not a separate role — too small a scope)
- Confidence labeling on every claim (settled / probable / open)
- **Estonian quote handling: verbatim original + English gloss bracket**. Hard rule. Briefly §6 has three load-bearing Estonian passages (Kaie §6.1, Peterson Juridica §6.2, Peterson demand §6.4) that MUST stay verbatim — back-translating changes the analytical object.
- Holding-silent rule — team produces analysis; PO does NOT engage Peterson/Kaie thread until memo lands AND jurist conversation happens
- Haapsalu-Suvekool repo READ-ONLY (cross-team context only)

## Workdir + deployment

- New GitHub repo `ESL-legal` (account TBD — **Q for PO at submission**)
- Local workdir: `~/Documents/github/ESL/legal/` (does NOT yet exist)
- Runtime team: `~/.claude/teams/esl-legal/`
- 8-file onboarding package mirroring S23 (roster, common-prompt, startup, design-spec, 6 prompts)
- **All-opus** model tier per S23. Every role is judgment-heavy; no haiku fan-out scenarios.

## Questions for PO before I draft prompts

1. **Names: Roman-jurist primary, or want EE-jurist variant (B)?** My strong recommendation: Roman.
2. **Architecture: Option B?** (6-char with EE/EU asymmetric workload)
3. **Adversary veto-weight: Editor MUST-address each `[ADVERSARY-FLAG]` (my preferred), or MAY-address?**
4. **GitHub account for `ESL-legal` repo** — TBD per your brief; need at submission time.

Sending this as your initial 4-question gate, matching the S23 workflow. Standing by for PO decisions before drafting the package.
